title: Bwatoo: Gender and noun classifiers
introduction:
General background. In many languages, “gender” can change how words around a noun look (for example, agreement on adjectives or pronouns), and some languages use “classifiers” with numerals or measure words (for example, a classifier chosen by the noun’s type). English has no classifier system, and gender mainly appears in third‑person pronouns (he, she, it; they). English adjectives (big, blue) and verbs do not change form for gender, and numerals combine directly with nouns (three books) without classifiers.
About English. Examples: She is tall; He is tall (same adjective). Three houses (no classifier). My mother, my uncle (gender is part of the noun’s meaning, not any agreement).
Preview for Bwatoo. According to the provided materials, Bwatoo lacks grammatical gender and has no noun‑classifier system. Gender is expressed lexically (e.g., dedicated nouns for ‘woman’, ‘man’, ‘grandma’, ‘grandpa’, ‘mother’, ‘uncle’). Numerals and quantifiers combine directly with nouns. Particles such as **ni**, **ani**, and **ne** function as relational/linking markers and possessives, not as classifiers. One document additionally notes a possible animate vs. inanimate opposition in the singular (-a vs. -n), but our sentence‑level data do not provide clear examples; we flag this as a point to watch.

sections.focus: No grammatical gender: pronouns and predicates do not change by sex

example.target_sentence: a nya thing ani xhapwen ma zho tha-nyaman
example.source_sentence: He gave me the fruits to try.
example.description: Third person is **a** with no male/female distinction; nothing in the clause changes for gender.
Predicate **suka** ‘sweet’ takes plural **-le** indexing plurality, not gender or noun class.
Descriptors **vunye-a** ‘blue’ and **hnabo-a** ‘thick’ do not change for gender; the -a here is not gender.
Possessive **ne-ong** ‘my’ shows person/number, not gender; no agreement by noun’s sex.
Possessive **ne-go** ‘your’ has no gender. Person/number clitics like **-ong**, **-go**, **-le** index participants/number, not gender.

sections.description: Across the data, there is no grammatical gender. Third‑person reference uses **a** without male/female contrast, and predicates/adjectives do not inflect for gender. Plural clitic **-le** marks plurality only. Possessives formed with **ne-** plus person clitics (e.g., **ne-ong**, **ne-go**) track person/number, never gender.

sections.focus: Gender is lexical (encoded by the noun), not by agreement

example.description: Female sex is encoded lexically by **nyanya** ‘mother’; **ne-ong** ‘my’ does not mark gender.
Male sex is encoded lexically by **hmii** ‘uncle’; no other word changes for gender.

example.target_sentence: ao ca walabe ka gee ca ju-thi
example.source_sentence: Grandpa here on the left, and Grandma on the right.
example.description: Male/female are lexical: **ao** ‘grandpa’, **gee** ‘grandma’; surrounding material shows no gender agreement.

example.target_sentence: xaabuit, tramo, thapia, ni ma xhoomu-le, meenan ni xaabuit
example.source_sentence: men and women, children, old people, entire families…
example.description: Sex and age distinctions are in the nouns themselves (**tramo** ‘women’, **xaabuit** ‘men/people’ by context); there is no grammatical gender agreement.

example.target_sentence: je papang ani xhwiaman nya ko 200 xaabuit
example.source_sentence: we’ll have to prepare enough food for two hundred people.
example.description: Human plurality is a lexical noun (**xaabuit** ‘people’); there is no gender agreement on other words.

sections.description: Sex/gender distinctions in Bwatoo are carried by specific nouns such as **tramo** ‘women’, **xaabuit** ‘men/people’ (context‑dependent), **gee** ‘grandma’, **ao** ‘grandpa’, **nyanya** ‘mother’, **hmii** ‘uncle’. No adjective, verb, or pronoun changes form in response to a noun’s sex.

sections.focus: No noun classifiers: numerals and quantifiers combine directly with nouns

example.target_sentence: je papang ani xhwiaman nya ko 200 xaabuit
example.source_sentence: we’ll have to prepare enough food for two hundred people.
example.description: The numeral phrase **ko 200 xaabuit** is formed without a classifier.

example.target_sentence: ka ngabe bo nya thi-u nyu
example.source_sentence: and we’ll give you some fish.
example.description: ‘Some fish’ appears as bare **nyu**; no classifier is used.

example.target_sentence: gau bo nya thi-bu ani dooxaman
example.description: Quantity is expressed via context and linkers; there is no noun‑class‑specific classifier with **ani dooxaman** ‘(some) vegetables’.

example.target_sentence: meenan ni xaabuit fe-nyima-le ni mata ma ni vila
example.description: Independent quantifier **meenan** ‘everyone’ and nouns occur without classifiers; **ni** links broadly (see next section).

sections.description: There is no numeral or measure classifier system. Numerals and quantity words combine directly with nouns (e.g., **ko 200 xaabuit** ‘200 people’; bare **nyu** ‘(some) fish’). Quantification is handled by independent items such as **meenan** ‘everyone’, not by a dedicated set of noun classifiers.

sections.focus: Particles ni, ani, and ne are general linkers/relational markers, not classifiers

example.target_sentence: meenan ni xaabuit fe-nyima-le ni mata ma ni vila
example.description: **ni** occurs before humans (**ni xaabuit**) and non‑humans (**ni mata**, **ni vila**), showing it is a general linker, not a class‑restricted classifier.

example.target_sentence: gau bo nya thi-bu ani dooxaman
example.description: **ani** functions as a general relational/linker before **dooxaman** ‘vegetables’; there is no evidence of a classifier category.
**ne-** marks possession (here **ne-ong** ‘my’) and attaches across many heads; it is not a classifier.
Same **ne-** possessive pattern with **ne-go** ‘your’; again, no classifier behavior.

example.target_sentence: a nya thing ani xhapwen ma zho tha-nyaman
example.source_sentence: He gave me the fruits to try.
example.description: **ani** links to **xhapwen** here; the same marker appears with many noun types, not a restricted semantic class.

sections.description: The wide distribution of **ni**, **ani**, and **ne** across human and non‑human nouns, activities, and other referents shows these are general linkers/relational markers (including possessive **ne-**), not noun classifiers.

sections.focus: Possession morphology is not a classifier system (dependent vs. relator nouns)

example.description: Possession with **ne-** + person clitic (**ne-ong**) expresses ‘my’, with no gender or classifier behavior.
Same possessive strategy with kin terms; gender is lexical (**hmii** ‘uncle’).
**ne-** attaches freely to varied heads (here **ti** ‘book’), not tied to any noun class.
**ne-** marks possession uniformly (**ne-go** ‘your’), regardless of the possessed noun’s semantics.

sections.description: Documented morphology adds that some nouns are “dependent” and take possessive suffixes directly (e.g., **jia-n** ‘his/her belly’, **duu-n** ‘his/her bone’, **vuu-n** ‘his/her hair’, **thithuu-jiia-n** ‘his/her constipation’), while others require a relator before the possessive suffix (e.g., **booma ka-n** ‘his/her shadow’, **bwahmo ka-n** ‘his/her grave’). These relational patterns (including relators like **ka-**, **koo-**, **pala-**) concern possession and association, not noun classes. The sets of nouns using a given relator are not homogeneous semantically. None of this constitutes a classifier system.

sections.focus: Note on animacy (-a vs. -n) and a source divergence

example.description: The form -a occurs here on descriptors, but our data do not tie it to animacy or noun class; no agreement alternation is visible.
Number marking (**-le**) is attested; no gender or animacy agreement is visible in this predicate example.

sections.description: Documents report a possible animate vs. inanimate opposition in the singular on nouns (and sometimes verbs), with **-a** (animate) vs. **-n** (inanimate). However, the sentence‑level data provided here do not supply clear examples of this opposition. The sentence analysis explicitly concludes there is no grammatical gender or classifier system, and it does not present animacy‑driven alternations. We therefore flag this as a possible morphological pattern (per the documents) that remains unattested in the sentence data above.

conclusion: What to remember: Bwatoo has no grammatical gender and no noun‑classifier system. Sex distinctions are encoded by specific nouns (e.g., ‘mother’, ‘uncle’, ‘grandma’, ‘grandpa’, ‘women’, ‘men/people’), not by agreement on other words. Numerals and quantifiers combine directly with nouns. Particles **ni**, **ani**, and **ne** function as general linkers/relational markers (including possession with **ne-**), not as classifiers. Person/number clitics like **-ong**, **-go**, **-le** index participants/number only. One document notes a possible singular animate/inanimate -a/-n opposition, but our sentence data do not demonstrate it; treat this as a point for further verification.
